name: Pectoralis Major
landmarks: Lateral third of Clavicle (palpate along the clavicle from the sternum laterally — the lateral third is roughly where the deltopectoral groove begins)
Coracoid Process (palpate below the lateral clavicle — feel for a bony bump under the anterior deltoid by pressing posteriorly into the deltopectoral triangle)
Sternal Border (palpate the lateral edge of the sternum — the pectoralis major attaches along this bony ridge)
placement: Position the patient supine with the arm abducted 30-45 degrees and externally rotated slightly to open the chest.
Identify the lateral third of the clavicle by palpating from the sternoclavicular joint laterally.
For the clavicular head: insert the needle 2-3 cm inferior to the lateral clavicle, angled slightly caudally at about 30 degrees to the skin surface.
Advance the needle 1-2 cm into the muscle belly — you should feel the firm resistance of muscle tissue.
For the sternal head: palpate the bulk of the muscle mid-chest, approximately 3-4 cm lateral to the sternal border at the level of the 3rd-4th rib.

Insert the needle TANGENTIALLY to the chest wall (15-30° from the skin, NEVER perpendicular) and aim the needle path OVER a rib, never over an intercostal space. The rib acts as a hard backstop and protects against pleural puncture.
Under ultrasound, confirm the needle tip is in pec major and the rib's bright cortical line is directly deep to the needle path before injecting.
Aspirate before injecting to confirm you are not intravascular. Distribute the dose across 2-3 sites in the muscle belly.
setup: Ultrasound (USG) is highly recommended for this injection.
Visualize the Pectoralis Major overlying the Pectoralis Minor — the injection target is the superficial muscle belly only.
Use a high-frequency linear probe placed transversely across the chest wall to identify the two layers.
Avoid the rib interspaces to prevent pneumothorax — always confirm needle depth with ultrasound.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz), Samsung LA3-16AD recommended
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the anterior chest wall, lateral to the sternal border at the 3rd-4th rib level
viewSteps: Position the patient supine with the arm slightly abducted. Place the probe transversely on the anterior chest.
The Pectoralis Major appears as the broad, flat superficial muscle layer directly under the subcutaneous fat — it spans most of the anterior chest.
Deep to the Pectoralis Major, a thin hyperechoic fascial plane separates it from the Pectoralis Minor.
The Pectoralis Minor appears as a smaller triangular muscle deep to the fascial plane.
CRITICAL: The Lateral Pectoral Nerve (and associated vessel) runs in the fascial plane between Pectoralis Major and Minor — look for a small hyperechoic dot with an adjacent vessel.
Deep to Pectoralis Minor, the hyperechoic pleural line and ribs are visible — the lung appears as a bright sliding line with comet-tail artifacts.
Confirm by having the patient adduct the arm against resistance — Pec Major will contract.
safetyNotes: PNEUMOTHORAX RISK: The lung lies deep to the pectoralis muscles, separated only by a thin layer of intercostal muscle in the spaces between ribs. NEVER use a perpendicular needle approach. Always angle the needle tangentially to the chest wall and aim the trajectory OVER a rib (which acts as a backstop), never over an intercostal space.

The brachial plexus and axillary vessels run in the axilla just lateral to pec major — do not let the needle drift laterally into the axillary fossa. Use color Doppler to map the axillary artery before injection.
The Lateral Pectoral Nerve runs between Pec Major and Minor — avoid the inter-fascial plane.
Use real-time US guidance to keep the needle within the Pec Major muscle belly at all times.
The thoracoacromial artery runs in the deltopectoral groove — use color Doppler laterally.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Pectoralis Major and Minor spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, arm abducted 30°. Place probe transversely on anterior chest, lateral to sternum at 3rd-4th rib. Photo from patient's right side showing probe on chest wall.
pearls: Start with low doses (25-50U Botox per site) — the pec major is a large muscle and easy to over-inject
Avoid injecting too medially near the sternum to prevent spread to intercostals
A spastic pec major is the #1 cause of shoulder internal rotation/adduction posture
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Total across both heads. Split dose between clavicular and sternal heads (e.g. 50-75U each). 200U upper bound exceeds typical consensus.

name: Latissimus Dorsi
landmarks: Inferior Angle of Scapula (palpate the lowest bony point of the scapula with the patient's arm at the side — the lat dorsi wraps around just below here)
Posterior Axillary Fold (grasp the fold of skin/muscle behind the armpit — the latissimus dorsi forms this fold)
Iliac Crest (palpate the superior rim of the pelvis posterolaterally — the muscle originates broadly from this structure)
placement: Position the patient in lateral decubitus with the affected side up, or prone with arm slightly abducted.
Grasp the posterior axillary fold between your thumb and fingers to identify the muscle bulk.
Palpate the Latissimus Dorsi — it is the lateral and superficial muscle forming the fold (Teres Major is deeper and more superior).
Identify the injection site approximately 4-5 cm inferior to the axillary crease along the mid-axillary line.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the mid-belly of the muscle.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle substance; you should feel firm muscular resistance.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the total dose across 2-3 sites along the muscle belly.
setup: USG is helpful to distinguish Latissimus Dorsi from Teres Major and Serratus Anterior.
Patient positioning in lateral decubitus with affected side up provides the best access.
Have the patient adduct the arm against resistance to confirm palpation of the correct muscle.
The muscle becomes tendinous as it approaches the intertubercular groove — inject into the fleshy belly, not the tendon.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posterior axillary fold, perpendicular to the long axis of the muscle fibers
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue superficially as a hyperechoic layer.
The Latissimus Dorsi appears as a broad, flat hypoechoic muscle with a bright fascial envelope — it is the most superficial muscle in the posterior axillary fold.
Deep to the Latissimus Dorsi, identify the Teres Major as a separate hypoechoic muscle belly separated by a fascial plane.
Further deep and anteriorly, the Serratus Anterior may be seen as thin hypoechoic slips overlying the ribs.
safetyNotes: The thoracodorsal nerve and artery run along the deep surface of the Latissimus Dorsi — use color Doppler to identify the vascular bundle.
Avoid the axillary neurovascular bundle by staying inferior and posterior to the axillary crease.
The long thoracic nerve runs along the Serratus Anterior — do not inject too deep.

probePlacementHint: Patient lateral decubitus or prone. Place probe transversely across posterior axillary fold. Photo from behind showing probe on lateral back/axilla.
pearls: Often co-injected with pec major and teres major for shoulder adduction/IR pattern
The posterior axillary fold is your best landmark — inject into the muscle belly as it crosses the fold
Lat dorsi contribution to spasticity is underappreciated — always assess it in shoulder posturing
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Biceps Brachii
landmarks: Coracoid Process (palpate below the lateral clavicle — feel for a bony prominence deep to the anterior deltoid; the short head originates here)
Acromion (palpate the bony shelf at the top of the shoulder — follow the spine of the scapula laterally to its tip)
Cubital Fossa (the triangular depression on the anterior elbow — bounded by brachioradialis laterally and pronator teres medially)
placement: Position the patient supine or seated with the arm supinated and elbow extended, resting on a support surface.
Identify the mid-point of the humerus on the anterior arm (halfway between the acromion and lateral epicondyle).
Palpate the biceps muscle belly — ask the patient to flex the elbow slightly against resistance to confirm.
Insert the needle into the bulk of the muscle belly at the mid-humeral level, perpendicular to the skin surface.
Advance 1-1.5 cm into the muscle — the biceps is a superficial muscle, so you do not need to go deep.
Stay superficial to the Brachialis muscle (which lies deep against the humerus).
Aspirate before injecting. You may inject both the long and short heads if needed, spacing injections 2-3 cm apart.
setup: Surface anatomy is usually sufficient for this injection given the prominent muscle belly.
USG confirms depth to avoid the brachial neurovascular bundle, which runs medially in the arm.
Have the patient flex the elbow against resistance to identify the muscle belly before marking.
The musculocutaneous nerve enters the muscle from the lateral side — stay in the central belly.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the anterior arm at the mid-humeral level
viewSteps: Position the patient supine with the arm slightly abducted and externally rotated. Place the probe transversely on the anterior arm.
The Biceps Brachii (short and long heads) appears as the large superficial hypoechoic muscle — it is the most prominent structure in the anterior compartment.
CRITICAL: The Musculocutaneous Nerve appears as a small hyperechoic oval within or at the deep border of the biceps — it typically sits between the biceps and brachialis muscles.
Deep to the biceps, the Brachialis muscle wraps around the anterior humerus.
The Radial Nerve may be visible laterally in the deeper plane, between the brachialis and the lateral intermuscular septum.
To distinguish short from long head: the short head is medial, the long head is lateral — a fascial septum often separates them.
safetyNotes: The Musculocutaneous Nerve runs between the biceps and brachialis — inject into the superficial belly of the biceps only.
The brachial artery and vein run on the medial side of the arm — avoid the medial approach.
The median nerve also runs medially — use an anterior or lateral approach for safety.
ultrasound.videoSource: Dr. Andrei Bursuc — Short head of biceps spasticity (Annotated US)

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, arm supinated. Place probe transversely on anterior mid-upper arm. Photo from lateral view showing probe across biceps belly.
pearls: One of the most commonly injected muscles in upper limb spasticity
Differentiate from brachialis — biceps is superficial and has a characteristic double-belly on US
If elbow flexion persists after biceps injection, the brachialis is likely the bigger contributor
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Brachialis
landmarks: Distal Humerus (palpate the anterior arm distally — feel the bony shaft of the humerus just proximal to the elbow)
Biceps Tendon (palpate the firm cord in the antecubital fossa during resisted flexion — the brachialis lies deep to this)
Lateral Epicondyle (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral elbow — the brachialis inserts on the coronoid process just proximal to this level)
placement: Position the patient supine or seated with the elbow slightly flexed (about 30 degrees) and the forearm supinated.

Use an ANTERIOR approach through the biceps muscle belly in the distal third of the arm — this avoids both the medial neurovascular bundle and the radial nerve, which dives around the lateral humerus into the spiral groove.
Palpate the biceps muscle belly in the distal third of the arm (about 4-6 cm proximal to the elbow crease).
Under ultrasound, identify biceps as the superficial muscle and brachialis as the deeper hypoechoic muscle directly on the humeral cortex.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin through the biceps belly, advancing into the brachialis below it.
Confirm the needle tip is in brachialis (deep) and not biceps (superficial) before injecting.
Stop advancing just before contacting bone — the target is the muscle belly, not the periosteum.
Aspirate before injecting to rule out intravascular placement.
setup: USG is MANDATORY for brachialis injection — the radial nerve courses around the lateral humerus and is at risk with any lateral approach.
Use an anterior in-plane approach through biceps. The needle should be visible on US the entire time.
Ensure the needle tip is in the muscular plane deep to the biceps but superficial to the humerus.
The brachial artery and median nerve lie medial — keep the needle in the central/anterior plane.
Consider EMG guidance as an adjunct to confirm placement in the correct muscle.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the anterior distal third of the arm, ~4-6 cm proximal to the elbow crease (matching the anterior-through-biceps approach)
viewSteps: Place the probe transversely across the anterior distal third of the arm, ~4-6 cm proximal to the elbow crease.
Identify subcutaneous tissue and the superficial hyperechoic fascia.
The Biceps Brachii is visible as a superficial oval hypoechoic muscle belly with a central tendon distally.
Deep to the Biceps, the Brachialis appears as a flattened hypoechoic muscle directly against the hyperechoic cortex of the distal humerus.
The bright cortical line of the humerus serves as your deep landmark — stop the needle before reaching it.
Scan laterally to identify the radial nerve in the spiral groove (small hyperechoic honeycomb) so you know where it is — your anterior approach should keep you well away from it.
safetyNotes: The radial nerve courses along the lateral and posterior humerus in the spiral groove — always identify it before needle insertion.
The brachial artery and median nerve lie medially — a lateral approach minimizes risk to these structures.
Avoid contacting the periosteum of the humerus, which can cause significant pain.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, elbow slightly flexed. Place probe transversely on lateral distal arm, 2-3 cm above lateral epicondyle. Photo showing probe on anterolateral distal arm.
pearls: Often the primary elbow flexor in spasticity — don't skip it just because biceps is more famous
Lies deep to the biceps — must see both muscles on US to inject the correct one
Higher doses often needed here vs biceps since brachialis is the primary elbow flexor
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Quadratus Lumborum
landmarks: Iliac Crest (palpate the superior bony rim of the pelvis posteriorly — the QL originates from the medial lip of this crest)
12th Rib (palpate the lowest rib posterolaterally — it is shorter and more mobile than the ribs above; the QL inserts here)
L3-L4 Transverse Processes (deep bony landmarks not directly palpable — approximate their location 3-4 cm lateral to the midline spinous processes)
placement: Position the patient prone with a pillow under the abdomen to reduce lumbar lordosis.
Palpate the iliac crest posteriorly and the 12th rib to define the boundaries of the muscle.
Identify the lateral border of the erector spinae muscle mass (the paraspinal bulk flanking the spine).
The injection site is just superior to the iliac crest and lateral to the erector spinae, approximately 4-5 cm lateral to the midline.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin, angling slightly medially toward the transverse processes of L3/L4.
Advance slowly under ultrasound guidance — the QL lies anterior to the erector spinae and posterior to the kidney.
Target depth is typically 3-5 cm depending on body habitus. Confirm the needle tip is within the muscle before injecting.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites within the muscle belly.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — retroperitoneal organs (kidneys) lie deep to this muscle and are at risk of injury.
Ensure the needle tip is visualized at all times during the injection.
A curvilinear probe may be needed in patients with larger body habitus.
Consider using an in-plane needle approach for continuous visualization of the needle path.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (axial) across the flank, just superior to the iliac crest, lateral to the erector spinae
viewSteps: Identify the subcutaneous fat and thoracolumbar fascia superficially as hyperechoic layers.
The Erector Spinae group appears as a large hypoechoic muscle mass medially, adjacent to the spinous processes.
Lateral and deep to the erector spinae, the Quadratus Lumborum appears as a smaller quadrilateral hypoechoic muscle connecting the iliac crest to the 12th rib.
Deep to the QL, identify the bright hyperechoic transverse processes of the lumbar vertebrae and the psoas major muscle.
Anterior to the QL (and deep), the kidney may be visible as a large ovoid structure with a bright capsule — this is your danger zone.
safetyNotes: The kidneys lie anterior and deep to the QL — always identify the anterior border of the muscle and do not advance beyond it.
The subcostal nerve (T12) runs along the deep surface of the QL — watch for it as a small hyperechoic structure.
Lumbar arteries travel near the transverse processes — use color Doppler to identify them before injection.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, pillow under abdomen. Place curvilinear probe transversely on flank, just above iliac crest. Photo from side showing probe on lateral flank.
pearls: Use curvilinear probe — this muscle is deep and linear probes often can't penetrate
Critical for lateral trunk flexion spasticity and pelvic obliquity
The kidney lies anterior — always identify it on US before injecting
supplies: Ultrasound machine with curvilinear probe (5-8 MHz, for deep muscles)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Gluteus Maximus
landmarks: PSIS (Posterior Superior Iliac Spine — palpate the bony dimple on each side of the low back, just lateral to the sacrum)
Greater Trochanter (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral hip — have the patient internally/externally rotate the hip to feel it roll under your fingers)
Ischial Tuberosity (palpate the bony prominence at the bottom of the buttock — most prominent with the hip flexed; the 'sitting bone')
placement: Position the patient prone or in lateral decubitus with the affected side up.
Palpate the PSIS (the bony dimple near the sacrum) and the Greater Trochanter (lateral bony prominence).

⚠️ NOTE: The 'upper-outer quadrant' rule from IM-injection teaching targets gluteus MEDIUS — it is NOT the gluteus maximus belly. For botulinum toxin into gluteus maximus, target the thick muscle belly inferior and posterior to that line, between the sacrum/coccyx and the greater trochanter.
Under ultrasound, identify the thick muscle belly of gluteus maximus posteriorly and inferiorly to gluteus medius. Stay superficial-to-mid depth.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the muscle bulk at multiple sites distributed across the belly (3-4 sites for a large muscle).
Advance 3-5 cm into the large muscle bulk (depth varies with body habitus).
Aspirate before injecting.

Stay LATERAL to a line drawn from the PSIS to the ischial tuberosity — the sciatic nerve exits the greater sciatic foramen medial to this line and dives deep beneath piriformis. Use color Doppler to identify the inferior gluteal vessels.
setup: USG placement ensures proper depth and avoids intrafascial injection.
The sciatic nerve runs deep to this muscle in the lower quadrants — always inject in the upper-outer quadrant.
This is a large, thick muscle — consider distributing the dose across 3-4 sites.
Have the patient contract the gluteus (squeeze buttocks together) to confirm the correct muscle before marking.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the upper-outer quadrant of the buttock
viewSteps: Identify the thick subcutaneous fat layer, which can be substantial in this region.
The Gluteus Maximus appears as a large, thick hypoechoic muscle with characteristic coarse fiber texture and bright fascial borders.
Deep to the Gluteus Maximus, identify the hyperechoic fascial plane separating it from the deeper Gluteus Medius.
The iliac bone or greater trochanter may be visible as a bright hyperechoic cortical line with posterior acoustic shadowing at depth.
In the lower quadrants, the sciatic nerve may be visible as a large hyperechoic flattened oval structure — confirm it is not in your injection path.
safetyNotes: The sciatic nerve exits the greater sciatic foramen deep to the piriformis and runs deep to the gluteus maximus — always inject in the upper-outer quadrant.
The superior and inferior gluteal arteries run deep to the muscle — aspirate before injection.
In thin patients the iliac bone is close to the surface — avoid periosteal injection.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone. Place curvilinear probe transversely on upper-outer quadrant of buttock. Photo from behind showing probe position on buttock.
pearls: Large muscle requiring higher total doses spread across multiple injection sites
Spastic glute max impairs sitting posture and hip flexion for transfers
The sciatic nerve runs deep — stay in the superficial/mid portion of the muscle belly
supplies: Ultrasound machine with curvilinear probe (5-8 MHz, for deep muscles)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Piriformis
landmarks: PSIS (Posterior Superior Iliac Spine — palpate the bony dimple on each side of the low back, at the level of S2)
Greater Trochanter (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral hip — the piriformis inserts on its superior-medial surface)
Sacral Hiatus (palpate the small bony depression at the distal sacrum/proximal coccyx in the midline — the piriformis originates from the anterior sacrum)
placement: Position the patient prone with a pillow under the pelvis to slightly flex the hips.
Palpate the PSIS and the Greater Trochanter — draw a line connecting these two landmarks.
Identify the midpoint of this PSIS-to-Greater-Trochanter line — this is your injection site.
The piriformis lies deep to the Gluteus Maximus at this location.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin surface at the midpoint.
Advance through the Gluteus Maximus (typically 4-6 cm depending on body habitus) until the needle tip enters the piriformis.
You may feel a subtle change in tissue resistance as you pass through the fascial plane between the two muscles.
Aspirate before injecting. Inject slowly and monitor for any sciatic symptoms (radiating pain down the leg).
setup: USG guidance is ESSENTIAL — the sciatic nerve exits the pelvis immediately inferior to the piriformis belly.
EMG guidance may be used as an adjunct to confirm needle placement within the piriformis.
If the patient reports radiating pain down the leg during needle advancement, STOP and reposition — you may be near the sciatic nerve.
The piriformis is a relatively small, deep muscle — precise needle placement is critical.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse along the line from PSIS to Greater Trochanter, centered at the midpoint
viewSteps: Identify the subcutaneous tissue and the thick Gluteus Maximus as a large superficial hypoechoic muscle.
Deep to the Gluteus Maximus, identify the hyperechoic fascial plane — the piriformis lies just below this.
The Piriformis appears as a smaller, flat hypoechoic muscle running obliquely from the sacrum toward the greater trochanter.
Deep to the piriformis, the hyperechoic bony landmarks of the posterior ilium/sacrum provide a deep boundary.
The sciatic nerve appears as a large hyperechoic oval structure (honeycomb pattern on cross-section) immediately inferior to the piriformis.
safetyNotes: The sciatic nerve exits immediately inferior to the piriformis — always identify it on ultrasound before injecting.
The inferior gluteal artery runs near the inferior border of the piriformis — use color Doppler to locate it.
The pudendal nerve and internal pudendal vessels exit medial to the piriformis — avoid directing the needle medially.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided BTX injection for piriformis syndrome

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, pillow under pelvis. Place curvilinear probe transversely along PSIS-to-greater-trochanter line, at midpoint. Photo showing probe between PSIS and GT.
pearls: Notoriously difficult to isolate on US — confirm with EMG if available
Sciatic nerve runs immediately deep or through this muscle — use EMG guidance strongly recommended
Often co-injected with other hip external rotators for combined pattern
supplies: Ultrasound machine with curvilinear probe (5-8 MHz, for deep muscles)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Tensor Fasciae Latae (TFL)
landmarks: ASIS (Anterior Superior Iliac Spine — palpate the bony prominence at the front of the iliac crest; it is easily felt with the patient supine)
Greater Trochanter (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral hip — the TFL lies in the interval between the ASIS and this landmark)
placement: Position the patient supine or in lateral decubitus with the affected side up.
Palpate the ASIS — this is the sharp bony point at the front of the hip.
Move your palpating fingers 2-3 cm distal and slightly lateral from the ASIS.
The TFL muscle belly is palpable here as a small, distinct mass before it transitions into the IT band.
Ask the patient to internally rotate the hip against resistance to confirm the muscle belly under your fingers.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin surface directly into the palpated muscle belly.
Advance only 1-2 cm — the TFL is a superficial muscle and the deeper Gluteus Minimus lies beneath it.
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection site in the muscle belly is usually sufficient.
setup: The TFL is a superficial muscle — do not insert the needle too deep or you will enter the Gluteus Minimus.
Confirm the muscle by having the patient internally rotate the hip against resistance.
The IT band is a fascial structure, not a muscle — ensure you are proximal enough to be in the fleshy belly.
This is generally a straightforward injection that can often be done by palpation alone.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the anterolateral hip, 2-3 cm distal to the ASIS
viewSteps: Identify the subcutaneous fat as a superficial hyperechoic layer — it is usually thin in this region.
The TFL appears as a small, oval hypoechoic muscle belly with a bright fascial envelope, superficial and anterior.
Deep to the TFL, the Gluteus Minimus appears as a broader hypoechoic muscle.
Deep to the Gluteus Minimus, the hyperechoic cortex of the ilium or greater trochanter provides a bony landmark.
safetyNotes: The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve runs near the ASIS — avoid injecting too close to the ASIS to prevent nerve injury.
Do not advance past the TFL into the Gluteus Minimus, which is a separate muscle with different function.
The ascending branch of the lateral circumflex femoral artery runs deep to the TFL — aspirate before injecting.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine or lateral. Place probe transversely on anterolateral hip, 2-3 cm below ASIS. Photo showing probe just distal to ASIS on lateral hip.
pearls: Often a key contributor to hip flexion and abduction spasticity patterns
Small muscle — use conservative doses (25-50U Botox)
Spastic TFL presents with combined hip flexion + internal rotation and resists passive hip extension — assess in supine with the Thomas test
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Rectus Femoris
landmarks: AIIS (Anterior Inferior Iliac Spine — palpate 2 cm below the ASIS; this deep bony point is the origin of the rectus femoris direct head)
Superior Pole of Patella (palpate the top of the kneecap — the rectus femoris tendon inserts here as part of the quadriceps tendon)
placement: Position the patient supine with the knee extended and the thigh relaxed.
Identify the mid-thigh by measuring halfway between the ASIS and the superior pole of the patella.
The rectus femoris is the most superficial and central muscle of the anterior thigh at this level.
Palpate the muscle belly — ask the patient to extend the knee against resistance to confirm.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the bulk of the muscle belly at mid-thigh.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle, staying superficial to the Vastus Intermedius below.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites within the muscle belly if using higher doses.
setup: USG is recommended to distinguish the fascial septum between Rectus Femoris and the underlying Vastus Intermedius.
The Rectus Femoris is the only biarticular quadriceps muscle — it contributes to stiff-knee gait when spastic.
Surface anatomy is usually sufficient for identification, but USG helps confirm depth.
Have the patient extend the knee against resistance to identify the muscle bulk before marking.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the anterior mid-thigh
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the superficial fascia (fascia lata) as the most superficial hyperechoic layers.
The Rectus Femoris appears as a prominent oval hypoechoic muscle belly in the center of the anterior thigh, with a bright fascial envelope.
On either side, the Vastus Lateralis (lateral) and Vastus Medialis (medial) are visible as large hypoechoic muscles.
Deep to the Rectus Femoris, a bright hyperechoic fascial plane separates it from the Vastus Intermedius.
The Vastus Intermedius lies directly on the hyperechoic cortex of the femoral shaft.
safetyNotes: The femoral nerve, artery, and vein lie in the femoral triangle (medial proximal thigh) — avoid the proximal medial approach.
The descending branch of the lateral circumflex femoral artery runs between the rectus femoris and vastus lateralis — use color Doppler.
Ensure the injection is superficial to the fascial plane separating the RF from the Vastus Intermedius.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, knee extended. Place probe transversely on anterior mid-thigh. Photo from lateral view showing probe across anterior thigh.
pearls: The only quad that crosses the hip — contributes to both stiff knee gait AND hip flexion
Key target for stiff-knee gait pattern in stroke and TBI
Superficial and easy to identify — one of the most straightforward injections
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)
dosageNote: Primary stiff-knee gait target. 1-2 sites in proximal-mid muscle belly.

name: Vastus Medialis
landmarks: Adductor Tubercle (palpate the bony prominence on the medial femoral condyle — the VMO fibers originate from the adductor magnus tendon near here)
Patella (palpate the kneecap — the VMO inserts on its superomedial border with an oblique fiber orientation)
Sartorius (palpate the strap-like muscle crossing the medial thigh obliquely — the VMO lies deep and lateral to its distal portion)
placement: Position the patient supine with the knee extended and the thigh relaxed.
Identify the distal third of the medial thigh — the VMO is prominent here.
Palpate the characteristic 'teardrop' shape of the VMO just proximal and medial to the patella.
Ask the patient to extend the knee against resistance — the VMO will contract visibly and palpably.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the bulk of the VMO muscle belly.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle substance.
Aspirate before injecting. A single injection site in the mid-belly is usually sufficient.
setup: USG is optional but helpful to avoid the adductor canal neurovascular bundle, which lies deep to the Sartorius.
The VMO is superficial and easily identified by palpation in most patients.
Be aware that the saphenous nerve and descending genicular artery run in the adductor canal deep and medial to the VMO.
This injection is rarely performed in isolation — usually part of a broader quadriceps toxin strategy.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the distal medial thigh, just proximal to the patella
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the superficial fascia as hyperechoic layers.
The Sartorius appears as a thin, strap-like hypoechoic muscle superficially on the medial side.
Deep and lateral to the Sartorius, the Vastus Medialis appears as a large teardrop-shaped hypoechoic muscle with oblique fiber orientation.
Deep to the Sartorius and medial to the VMO, identify the adductor canal — the femoral artery appears as an anechoic pulsatile circle with color Doppler flow.
The hyperechoic cortex of the distal femur provides the deep bony landmark.
safetyNotes: The saphenous nerve and descending genicular artery travel in the adductor canal deep to the Sartorius — do not inject too medially or deeply.
The femoral artery transitions to the popliteal artery through the adductor hiatus in this region — use color Doppler.
The medial superior genicular artery runs near the distal VMO — aspirate before injection.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, knee extended. Place probe transversely on distal medial thigh, just above patella (VMO area). Photo showing probe on medial knee/thigh junction.
pearls: VMO dysfunction contributes to patellar tracking issues in spastic patients
Inject at the distal bulge (VMO) — most identifiable part
Rarely injected in isolation — usually part of a multi-muscle quad program
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Vastus Lateralis
landmarks: Greater Trochanter (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral hip — the VL originates from the intertrochanteric line and lateral femur distal to this)
Lateral Condyle of Femur (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral knee — the VL inserts via the quadriceps tendon and lateral retinaculum)
IT Band (palpate the taut band on the lateral thigh — the VL lies deep/anterior to this fascial structure)
placement: Position the patient supine or in lateral decubitus with the affected side up.
Identify the mid-to-distal lateral thigh (approximately the middle third of the femur length).
Palpate the IT band as a taut fascial band on the lateral thigh.
The injection target is deep and anterior to the IT band, into the muscle belly pressed against the femur.
Insert the needle through the IT band, angling slightly anteriorly to enter the VL muscle belly.
Advance 2-3 cm into the muscle substance until you feel the firm muscle against bone.
Avoid injecting into the IT band itself — it is a fascial structure and toxin will not be effective there.
Aspirate before injecting. Use multiple injection sites (2-3) along the length of the muscle for adequate coverage.
setup: Multiple injection sites are often needed along the length of the femur for this large muscle.
Ensure the needle passes through the IT band into actual muscle tissue.
The VL is a common intramuscular injection site (vaccinations) — familiarity with the anatomy is helpful.
USG can confirm that the needle is in muscle tissue and not in the IT band or subcutaneous fat.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral mid-thigh
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the fascia lata (including the IT band component) as hyperechoic superficial layers.
The Vastus Lateralis appears as a large hypoechoic muscle deep to the IT band, wrapping around the lateral femur.
The hyperechoic cortex of the femoral shaft is visible deep to the VL as a bright curved line with posterior shadowing.
Medially, the Vastus Intermedius and Rectus Femoris may be visible separated by fascial planes.
safetyNotes: The descending branch of the lateral circumflex femoral artery runs in the intermuscular septum between VL and RF — use color Doppler.
Perforating branches of the profunda femoris artery may cross the field — aspirate before injecting.
The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is more proximal and superficial — it is at risk only with very proximal injections.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine or lateral. Place probe transversely on lateral mid-thigh. Photo from lateral view showing probe on outer thigh.
pearls: Often contributes to knee extension spasticity more than rectus femoris
Large muscle belly — may need multiple injection points for adequate distribution
Safe injection zone is the lateral thigh — away from major neurovascular structures
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Vastus Intermedius
landmarks: Anterior Femur (not directly palpable — lies deep to the rectus femoris; approximate by palpating the mid-anterior thigh)
Deep to Rectus Femoris (the VI is only accessible by passing through or around the RF — the fascial septum between them is the key landmark)
placement: Position the patient supine with the knee extended and the thigh relaxed.
Identify the proximal-to-mid anterior thigh (the VI belly is largest in the proximal third).
Palpate the Rectus Femoris muscle belly — the Vastus Intermedius lies directly deep to it.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin through the center of the Rectus Femoris.
Advance through the RF (typically 1-2 cm) until you feel a subtle 'pop' through the fascial septum separating the two muscles.
Continue advancing into the VI muscle belly, which sits directly on the femoral shaft (total depth approximately 2-4 cm).
Stop before contacting the periosteum of the femur.
Aspirate before injecting. USG confirmation of needle position is mandatory.
setup: USG is MANDATORY to effectively distinguish the fascial plane between Rectus Femoris and Vastus Intermedius.
An in-plane needle approach under US allows real-time visualization of the needle crossing the fascial septum.
The VI is the deepest quadriceps muscle and cannot be reliably targeted by palpation alone.
This injection is often performed in conjunction with RF injection — plan your dose distribution accordingly.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the anterior proximal-to-mid thigh
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and fascia lata superficially.
The Rectus Femoris appears as the superficial oval hypoechoic muscle in the center of the field.
Deep to the RF, identify the bright hyperechoic fascial septum — this is your target plane to cross.
The Vastus Intermedius appears as a thinner, flatter hypoechoic muscle directly deep to this fascial septum.
The hyperechoic cortex of the femoral shaft lies immediately deep to the VI — this is your depth limit.
safetyNotes: The descending branch of the lateral circumflex femoral artery runs laterally between the muscles — use color Doppler before inserting.
Do not advance the needle to the periosteum of the femur, which causes pain and is not the target.
Ensure the needle crosses the fascial septum to confirm it is in the VI and not still in the RF.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, knee extended. Place probe transversely on anterior proximal thigh (same region as rectus femoris but deeper target). Photo from front showing probe on anterior thigh.
pearls: Deepest of the quadriceps — requires longer needle and confident US identification
Lies directly on the femur — use the femur as your deep landmark on US
Often co-injected when other quads alone don't adequately reduce extension tone
supplies: 22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Semimembranosus
landmarks: Ischial Tuberosity (palpate the bony prominence at the base of the buttock with the hip flexed — the semimembranosus originates here, lateral to the semitendinosus origin)
Medial Tibial Condyle (palpate the broad bony prominence on the medial proximal tibia — the semimembranosus inserts on its posterior surface)
placement: Position the patient prone with the knee slightly flexed (place a pillow under the ankle).
Identify the mid-thigh to distal thigh on the posterior-medial aspect.
Ask the patient to flex the knee against resistance — palpate the medial hamstrings.
The Semitendinosus is the superficial medial hamstring with a prominent tendon distally; the Semimembranosus is deep to it.
Approach from the medial side of the thigh to access the semimembranosus belly.
Insert the needle from the medial aspect, angling slightly posteriorly and laterally to pass deep to the Semitendinosus.
Advance 2-3 cm to reach the semimembranosus muscle belly.
Aspirate before injecting. Confirm placement with ultrasound.
setup: USG is helpful to distinguish the semimembranosus from the more superficial semitendinosus.
The semimembranosus has a broad, flat 'membranous' appearance on ultrasound, in contrast to the round semitendinosus.
Have the patient flex the knee against resistance to identify the hamstring group.
The sciatic nerve runs laterally in the posterior thigh — it is generally not at risk with a medial approach.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posterior medial thigh at mid-to-distal level
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the deep fascia as superficial hyperechoic layers.
The Semitendinosus appears as a round or oval hypoechoic muscle belly superficially (or its hyperechoic tendon distally).
Deep to the Semitendinosus, the Semimembranosus appears as a broader, flatter hypoechoic muscle with a characteristic 'membranous' aponeurosis.
The hyperechoic cortex of the femur may be visible deep and laterally.
The popliteal vessels may be visible distally in the popliteal fossa — identify them with color Doppler.
safetyNotes: The sciatic nerve runs in the lateral posterior thigh — a medial approach avoids it, but always verify its position.
The popliteal artery and vein lie deep in the popliteal fossa distally — avoid injecting too distally or deeply.
The saphenous nerve runs medially in the adductor canal — it may be at risk with very medial approaches.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, knee slightly flexed. Place probe transversely on posterior medial thigh at mid-to-distal level. Photo from behind showing probe on posteromedial thigh.
pearls: Broader, flatter muscle — differentiate from semitendinosus by its more lateral position distally
Key hamstring for medial knee flexion contracture patterns
Sciatic nerve runs in the posterior thigh — always identify it before injecting
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Semitendinosus
landmarks: Ischial Tuberosity (palpate the bony prominence at the base of the buttock with the hip flexed — the semitendinosus shares a common origin tendon with the biceps femoris here)
Medial Tibial Condyle (palpate the medial proximal tibia — the semitendinosus inserts on the anteromedial surface as part of the pes anserinus)
placement: Position the patient prone with the knee slightly flexed (place a pillow under the ankle).
Identify the mid-thigh on the directly posterior surface.
Ask the patient to flex the knee against resistance — palpate the medial hamstring group.
The Semitendinosus is the superficial medial hamstring; it often has a distinct raphe (tendinous inscription) in the mid-belly.
Palpate the round muscle belly — it becomes a long, cord-like tendon in the distal third.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the mid-belly of the muscle (proximal to the tendinous portion).
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle substance — it is relatively superficial.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: The semitendinosus is easily palpable in most patients.
Confirm the correct muscle by having the patient flex the knee against resistance — feel for the superficial medial cord.
Inject into the fleshy belly, not the distal tendon (which begins roughly at the junction of the middle and distal thirds).
USG is generally not required but can confirm you are in the correct muscle.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the posterior mid-thigh
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the deep fascia superficially.
The Semitendinosus appears as a round or oval hypoechoic muscle belly in the medial posterior thigh — it is the most superficial of the medial hamstrings.
A characteristic central hyperechoic raphe (tendinous inscription) may be seen within the muscle belly at mid-thigh.
Deep to the Semitendinosus, the broader Semimembranosus is visible as a flatter hypoechoic structure.
safetyNotes: The sciatic nerve runs laterally in the posterior thigh — it is generally safe with a medial injection approach.
The posterior femoral cutaneous nerve runs superficially in the midline — avoid the most superficial subcutaneous plane.
Distally, the popliteal vessels are at risk — do not inject in the distal third of the thigh.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, knee slightly flexed. Place probe transversely on posterior mid-thigh. Photo from behind showing probe on posterior thigh, medial side.
pearls: Round tendon easily palpated posteromedially — the muscle belly is proximal
Often has a ropy, cord-like tendon that's palpable behind the knee
Co-injection with semimembranosus gives better results than either alone for hamstring spasticity
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Gastrocnemius (Medial)
landmarks: Medial Femoral Condyle (palpate the bony prominence on the medial knee — the medial gastrocnemius head originates from its posterior surface)
Popliteal Fossa (palpate the diamond-shaped depression behind the knee — the gastrocnemius heads form the distal borders of this fossa)
placement: Position the patient prone with the feet hanging off the edge of the bed.
Identify the proximal third of the posterior calf — this is where the medial gastrocnemius belly is largest.
Palpate the large medial muscle belly — ask the patient to plantarflex the ankle against resistance to confirm.
The injection site is 2-3 cm distal to the popliteal crease, on the medial side of the calf midline.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the bulk of the medial gastrocnemius belly.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle — the gastrocnemius is superficial.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites within the medial belly if using higher doses.
setup: Avoid the midline of the calf to protect the Sural Nerve and small saphenous vein.
The medial head is larger than the lateral head — it is the primary contributor to equinus in most patients.
Prone positioning with feet off the table edge allows easy access and palpation.
This is one of the most commonly injected muscles in spasticity management — practice good technique here.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal medial posterior calf, 2-3 cm distal to the popliteal crease
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and the superficial fascia.
The medial Gastrocnemius appears as a large, thick hypoechoic muscle with a pennate fiber pattern and bright fascial borders.
Deep to the Gastrocnemius, identify the hyperechoic fascial plane separating it from the deeper Soleus muscle.
The Soleus appears as a hypoechoic muscle with a different (more complex) fiber architecture deep to the septum.
Between the two Gastrocnemius heads in the midline, the sural nerve and small saphenous vein may be visible.
safetyNotes: The sural nerve and small saphenous vein run in the midline between the two gastrocnemius heads — avoid the midline.
The popliteal artery and tibial nerve lie deep in the proximal popliteal fossa — do not inject too proximally or deeply.
The medial sural cutaneous nerve branches from the tibial nerve — it is superficial to the gastrocnemius fascia.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided M. Gastrocnemius BTX injection

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, feet off bed edge. Place probe transversely on proximal medial calf, 2-3 cm below popliteal crease. Photo from behind showing probe on medial proximal calf.
pearls: Most commonly injected muscle for equinovarus foot — start here
The medial head is larger and usually more spastic than the lateral head
Inject proximal — the muscle belly is short and becomes tendinous quickly
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Gastrocnemius (Lateral)
landmarks: Lateral Femoral Condyle (palpate the bony prominence on the lateral knee — the lateral gastrocnemius head originates from its posterior surface)
Fibular Head (palpate the small bony knob on the proximal lateral leg, just distal to the knee — the common peroneal nerve wraps around it)
placement: Position the patient prone with the feet hanging off the edge of the bed.
Identify the proximal third of the posterior calf on the lateral side.
Palpate the lateral gastrocnemius belly — it is smaller than the medial head.
The injection site is just medial to the fibular head, 2-3 cm distal to the popliteal crease.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the lateral muscle belly.
Advance 1-1.5 cm into the muscle substance.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: The lateral head is usually smaller in bulk than the medial head.
Avoid the Common Peroneal Nerve, which wraps around the fibular head — stay medial to the fibular head.
This injection is often combined with the medial gastrocnemius injection for equinus management.
USG can help distinguish the lateral gastrocnemius from the underlying soleus and plantaris.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal lateral posterior calf, 2-3 cm distal to the popliteal crease
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous fat and superficial fascia.
The lateral Gastrocnemius appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly — it is typically thinner and narrower than the medial head.
Deep to the lateral Gastrocnemius, the Soleus is visible separated by a hyperechoic fascial plane.
Laterally, the fibular head appears as a bright hyperechoic bony structure with posterior shadowing.
The common peroneal nerve may be visible as a small hyperechoic honeycomb structure wrapping around the fibular neck.
safetyNotes: The common peroneal nerve wraps around the fibular head — always identify its position and stay medial to the fibular head.
The sural nerve runs between the gastrocnemius heads — avoid the midline between the two heads.
The popliteal vessels are deep and proximal — do not inject too deeply in the proximal calf.
ultrasound.videoSource: Prof. Murat Karkucak - US guided M. Gastrocnemius BTX injection

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, feet off bed edge. Place probe transversely on proximal lateral calf, 2-3 cm below popliteal crease. Photo from behind showing probe on lateral proximal calf.
pearls: Smaller than medial head — use lower doses
Common peroneal nerve runs nearby at the fibular neck — stay distal and medial
Often co-injected with medial gastroc and soleus for equinus pattern
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Soleus (Medial)
landmarks: Medial Border of Tibia (palpate the sharp subcutaneous border of the tibia on the medial leg — the soleus originates from the posterior tibial surface just behind this edge)
Deep to Gastrocnemius (the soleus lies entirely deep to the gastrocnemius in the proximal calf — it extends more distally and is accessible where the gastrocnemius tapers)
placement: Position the patient prone with the feet hanging off the edge of the bed.
Palpate the medial border of the tibia in the mid-to-distal calf.
Move your palpating finger 1-2 cm posterior to the tibial border — this is where the soleus is accessible.
The injection site is distal to the bulk of the medial gastrocnemius belly (approximately mid-calf level).
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin, directed slightly laterally and posteriorly.
Advance 2-3 cm to pass deep to the Gastrocnemius and enter the Soleus muscle belly.
Under ultrasound, confirm the needle tip has crossed the fascial plane between the two muscles.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute the dose across 2 sites.
setup: USG is highly recommended to distinguish the fascial plane between Gastrocnemius (superficial) and Soleus (deep).
The soleus is a large muscle that extends more distally than the gastrocnemius — it is best accessed in the mid-to-distal calf.
An in-plane needle approach allows real-time visualization of the needle crossing into the soleus.
The posterior tibial neurovascular bundle runs deep to the soleus — do not advance too deeply.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the medial mid-calf, 1-2 cm posterior to the tibial border
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia.
The Gastrocnemius (or its aponeurosis distally) appears as a superficial hypoechoic layer with a bright fascial border.
Deep to the Gastrocnemius, a bright hyperechoic fascial septum marks the boundary with the Soleus.
The Soleus appears as a large hypoechoic muscle with a complex multipennate fiber architecture, distinctly different from the Gastrocnemius.
Deep to the Soleus, the posterior tibial vessels and tibial nerve may be visible — these are your deep boundary.
safetyNotes: The posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve run deep to the soleus in the deep posterior compartment — do not advance the needle through the soleus.
The saphenous vein and nerve run subcutaneously along the medial leg — avoid very superficial injury.
Peroneal vessels may be encountered laterally if the needle is directed too far lateral.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, feet off edge. Place probe transversely on medial mid-calf, just posterior to tibial border. Photo from behind/medial showing probe on mid-calf.
pearls: Deep to gastrocnemius — must angle the needle past gastroc on US
Key differentiator: gastroc crosses the knee, soleus doesn't. Test with knee flexed vs extended
If equinus persists with knee flexed, soleus is the primary contributor
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Soleus (Lateral)
landmarks: Fibula (palpate the lateral leg bone — the soleus originates from the posterior surface of the proximal fibular head and shaft)
Lateral Border of Gastrocnemius (palpate the lateral gastrocnemius belly — the lateral soleus is accessible just lateral/anterior to where the gastrocnemius tapers)
placement: Position the patient prone with the feet hanging off the edge of the bed.
Identify the lateral aspect of the mid-calf where the lateral Gastrocnemius belly tapers.
Palpate the lateral bulge of the Soleus — it is palpable just lateral and anterior to the lateral Gastrocnemius belly.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into this lateral soleus bulge at mid-calf level.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle belly.
Aspirate before injecting. Multiple distal injection sites may be used along the length of the muscle.
setup: Multiple distal injection sites may be needed to adequately address spasticity throughout this large muscle.
The lateral approach avoids the posterior tibial neurovascular bundle, which runs more medially.
USG can confirm needle placement within the soleus and not in the gastrocnemius or peroneal muscles.
The soleus is primarily a slow-twitch postural muscle — it often requires higher doses than the gastrocnemius.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the lateral mid-calf
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and superficial fascia.
The lateral Gastrocnemius (or its aponeurosis) appears as a superficial hypoechoic layer, tapering distally.
Deep and lateral, the Soleus appears as a broad hypoechoic muscle with a multipennate pattern.
The fibula appears as a bright hyperechoic cortical line laterally with posterior acoustic shadowing.
safetyNotes: The peroneal artery runs between the soleus and tibialis posterior in the deep compartment — use color Doppler.
The sural nerve runs superficially in the subcutaneous tissue of the posterior calf — avoid very superficial injection.
The common peroneal nerve wraps around the proximal fibula — stay distal to the fibular head.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, feet off edge. Place probe transversely on lateral mid-calf. Photo from behind showing probe on lateral mid-calf.
pearls: Less commonly injected than medial soleus but important for varus component
Posterior tibial vessels run between soleus and deep compartment — identify on US
Smaller volume than medial — adjust dosing accordingly
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Flexor Digitorum Longus (FDL)
landmarks: Medial Tibial Border (palpate the sharp subcutaneous edge of the tibia on the medial leg — the FDL lies on the posterior tibial surface just behind this border)
Posterior Tibial Artery Pulse (palpate behind the medial malleolus for the posterior tibial pulse — the FDL tendon runs adjacent to this neurovascular bundle distally)
placement: Position the patient prone or supine with the leg externally rotated to expose the medial calf.
Identify the distal third of the medial leg.
Palpate the medial border of the tibia — this is the sharp subcutaneous bony edge.
Attempt to palpate the posterior tibial pulse behind the medial malleolus to appreciate the neurovascular bundle location.
The injection site is just posterior to the medial tibial border in the distal third of the leg.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin, directing it toward the posterior aspect of the tibia and fibula.
Advance 2-3 cm under ultrasound guidance to enter the FDL muscle belly.
Aspirate before injecting. Confirm the needle is in the FDL and not in the neurovascular bundle.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — the posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve run immediately adjacent to this muscle.
Color Doppler should be used to identify the posterior tibial artery before needle insertion.
The FDL is a small, deep muscle — precise needle placement requires real-time imaging.
Have the patient flex the toes against resistance to confirm muscle identification on USG.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the medial distal leg, just posterior to the tibial border
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the superficial crural fascia.
The Gastrocnemius/Soleus complex is visible superficially as a hypoechoic muscle layer (or tendon distally).
Deep to the Soleus, the FDL appears as a small hypoechoic muscle belly lying along the posterior tibial surface.
Identify the posterior tibial artery (pulsatile anechoic circle) and tibial nerve (hyperechoic honeycomb) adjacent to the FDL.
The hyperechoic cortex of the posterior tibia provides the deep bony landmark.
safetyNotes: The posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve run immediately adjacent to the FDL — always identify them with color Doppler before injecting.
The FDL, tibialis posterior, and FHL all lie in the deep posterior compartment — confirm the correct muscle by asking the patient to flex the toes.
The peroneal artery runs laterally between the deep compartment muscles — do not direct the needle too laterally.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone or supine with leg externally rotated. Place probe transversely on medial distal leg, just behind tibial border. Photo showing probe on posteromedial distal leg.
pearls: Key target for toe curling/clawing in combination with FDB
Runs behind the medial malleolus — a tight FDL will pull toes into flexion during stance
Small muscle — conservative dosing (25-50U Botox per muscle)
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Flexor Digitorum Brevis (FDB)
landmarks: Calcaneus (palpate the bony heel — the FDB originates from the medial calcaneal tuberosity on the plantar surface)
Plantar Fascia (palpate the firm, taut band on the sole of the foot running from the heel toward the toes — the FDB lies deep to this structure)
placement: Position the patient prone with the foot hanging off the edge of the bed, or supine with the ankle dorsiflexed to expose the sole.
Palpate the calcaneal tuberosity on the plantar surface (the bony heel).
Measure 3-5 cm distal to the calcaneal tuberosity in the midline of the foot.
This is the injection site — the FDB belly is central in the plantar foot at this level.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the plantar skin surface.
Advance through the thick plantar fascia (you will feel firm resistance, then a give as you penetrate the fascia).
Enter the FDB muscle belly — typically 1-2 cm depth total.
Aspirate before injecting. Stay superficial to the deeper foot intrinsics (quadratus plantae, lumbricals).
setup: USG guidance is recommended to ensure the injection is deep to the plantar fascia but superficial to the deeper foot intrinsics.
The plantar skin is very thick and sensitive — consider topical anesthetic or ethyl chloride spray.
A 25-gauge or smaller needle is preferred given the sensitivity of the plantar surface.
Have the patient flex the toes to confirm the FDB belly on ultrasound before injecting.

ultrasound.orientation: Longitudinal (sagittal) along the midline of the plantar foot, from calcaneus toward the toes
viewSteps: Identify the thick hyperechoic plantar fascia as the most superficial fibrous layer on the sole.
Deep to the plantar fascia, the FDB appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly with a fibrillar pattern.
The calcaneal tuberosity is visible proximally as a bright hyperechoic cortical line — the FDB originates here.
Deep to the FDB, the quadratus plantae and lateral plantar neurovascular bundle may be visible.
safetyNotes: The medial and lateral plantar nerves and arteries run deep to the FDB — do not advance too deeply.
The plantar fascia is very thick — ensure the needle has passed through it into actual muscle tissue.
Avoid the medial plantar nerve, which runs between the FDB and abductor hallucis on the medial side.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, foot hanging off bed. Place probe longitudinally on plantar midfoot from calcaneus toward toes. Photo from plantar view showing probe on sole of foot.
pearls: Plantar injection — painful for patient, consider local anesthetic or ice first
Very superficial on the plantar surface — use short needle and minimal depth
Often co-injected with FDL and FHL for comprehensive toe flexion pattern

name: Tibialis Anterior
landmarks: Tibial Crest (palpate the sharp anterior ridge of the tibia — it is subcutaneous and runs the length of the shin; the tibialis anterior lies immediately lateral to it)
Lateral Condyle of Tibia (palpate the broad bony prominence on the lateral proximal tibia — the tibialis anterior originates from the lateral tibial condyle and upper tibial shaft)
placement: Position the patient supine with the leg extended and the ankle in neutral.
Identify the proximal third of the anterior leg.
Palpate the sharp anterior crest of the tibia — the prominent subcutaneous ridge on the shin.
Move your finger 2 cm lateral to the tibial crest — the tibialis anterior is the largest muscle bulk here.
Ask the patient to dorsiflex the foot against resistance to confirm the muscle belly under your fingers.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the largest muscle bulk, approximately 2 cm lateral to the tibial crest.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle belly.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: The tibialis anterior is superficial and easily identified — this is a straightforward injection.
Avoid the anterior tibial artery, which runs deep to the muscle near the interosseous membrane.
The deep peroneal nerve runs alongside the anterior tibial artery — it is generally protected by the muscle itself.

⚠️ STOP — DO NOT INJECT in typical UMN spasticity. The tibialis anterior is the primary ankle dorsiflexor and is almost always WEAK in upper-motor-neuron syndromes (it's the muscle that fails in foot drop). Injecting it will cause or worsen drop foot.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the proximal anterolateral leg, 2 cm lateral to the tibial crest
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue — it is thin on the anterior leg.
The Tibialis Anterior appears as a large hypoechoic muscle with a prominent central tendon in the distal portion, located immediately lateral to the tibial cortex.
The hyperechoic tibial cortex is visible medially as a bright flat line.
The Extensor Digitorum Longus is visible laterally, separated by a fascial septum.
Deep to the tibialis anterior, the interosseous membrane appears as a bright hyperechoic line connecting the tibia and fibula — the anterior tibial vessels run just anterior to this.
safetyNotes: The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve run deep to the muscle on the interosseous membrane — do not advance the needle to this depth.
The superficial peroneal nerve runs in the lateral compartment — avoid directing the needle too far laterally.
The tibial cortex is immediately medial — contacting the periosteum is painful; angle the needle slightly laterally.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, leg extended. Place probe transversely on proximal anterolateral leg, 2 cm lateral to tibial crest. Photo from front showing probe on anterior shin.
pearls: ⚠️ DO NOT INJECT in typical UMN spasticity — this muscle is almost always weak, not spastic. Injecting it causes foot drop.
Rare exception: focal dystonia with confirmed EMG over-activity in TA, with neurology / physiatry consultation. Even then, very low doses only.
Listed here for anatomic reference and to warn against injection. PROTECT this muscle's function for foot clearance during swing phase.
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Tibialis Posterior
landmarks: Medial Tibial Border (palpate the sharp subcutaneous tibial edge — the tibialis posterior lies on the posterior surface of the interosseous membrane, deep to the superficial posterior compartment muscles)
Fibular Head (palpate the bony knob on the proximal lateral leg — helps orient the interosseous membrane between tibia and fibula)
Intermalleolar Line (draw an imaginary line between the medial and lateral malleoli — the midpoint of the leg between the fibular head and this line approximates the mid-belly)
placement: Position the patient prone or supine with the leg externally rotated.
Identify the midpoint between the fibular head and the intermalleolar line on the medial leg.
Approach from the medial side of the leg, posterior to the medial tibial border.
Insert the needle directed posterolaterally, sliding along the posterior aspect of the tibia.
Advance through the superficial posterior compartment muscles (Gastrocnemius/Soleus) toward the interosseous membrane.
The tibialis posterior lies directly on the interosseous membrane — it is the deepest muscle in this compartment.
Typical depth is 3-5 cm depending on body habitus — USG confirms placement.
Aspirate before injecting. Confirm the needle is in the tibialis posterior and not in overlying muscles.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — high risk of posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve injury.
The tibialis posterior is the deepest muscle in the deep posterior compartment.
Use an in-plane needle approach for continuous visualization during this deep injection.
Have the patient invert the foot against resistance to confirm the muscle on ultrasound — this is the strongest foot invertor.

ultrasound.probe: High-frequency linear (10-15 MHz) in thin patients; lower-frequency linear (6-9 MHz) or curvilinear (5-8 MHz) is often required in average/larger patients due to muscle depth (3-5+ cm)
ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the medial mid-leg, posterior to the tibial border
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the crural fascia superficially.
The Gastrocnemius and Soleus form the superficial posterior compartment — they appear as hypoechoic muscle layers with a fascial septum between them.
Deep to the Soleus, the posterior tibial vessels (pulsatile anechoic circles) and tibial nerve (hyperechoic honeycomb) are visible in the deep posterior compartment.
The Tibialis Posterior appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly lying directly on the hyperechoic interosseous membrane, deep to the neurovascular bundle.
The bright cortical lines of the tibia (medially) and fibula (laterally) frame the compartment — the interosseous membrane connects them.
safetyNotes: The posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve run in the deep posterior compartment directly superficial to the tibialis posterior — always identify them with color Doppler.
The peroneal artery runs between the tibialis posterior and flexor hallucis longus laterally — use color Doppler.
This is a high-risk injection — do not attempt without real-time ultrasound guidance and confident identification of all neurovascular structures.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone or supine with leg externally rotated. Place probe transversely on medial mid-leg, just behind tibia. Photo from medial side showing probe behind tibial border.
pearls: The #1 cause of varus foot in spasticity — always assess this muscle
Deep compartment — longer needle needed. Use US to navigate past soleus
Posterior tibial artery runs alongside — identify it with color Doppler before injecting
supplies: Ultrasound machine with linear probe (high-frequency for thin patients; lower-frequency or curvilinear for deeper anatomy)
22-25G spinal needle (3.5 inch for deep muscles)

name: Flexor Hallucis Longus (FHL)
landmarks: Fibula — distal shaft (palpate the lateral leg bone in its distal third — the FHL originates from the posterior fibula and lies posteromedial to it)
Achilles Tendon (palpate the thick cord at the posterior ankle — the FHL belly lies deep and lateral in the distal calf, anterior to the Achilles)
placement: Position the patient prone with the feet hanging off the edge of the bed.
Identify the distal third of the posterior leg, lateral aspect.
The FHL belly is located posteromedial to the fibula, deep to the Soleus and lateral Gastrocnemius.
Approach from the lateral side to avoid the neurovascular bundle, which runs medially.
Insert the needle lateral to the Achilles tendon, directed toward the posterior fibula.
Advance 2-4 cm under ultrasound guidance to pass through the Soleus and enter the FHL belly.
Confirm placement by asking the patient to flex the great toe — you should see movement of the muscle on ultrasound.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: USG is MANDATORY — the FHL is a deep muscle and the neurovascular bundle runs medially.
A lateral approach minimizes risk to the posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve.
Have the patient flex the great toe against resistance to identify the FHL on ultrasound.
The FHL is smaller than the FDL — use precise dosing.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the distal posterolateral calf, adjacent to the fibula
viewSteps: Identify subcutaneous tissue and the Soleus muscle/aponeurosis superficially.
The fibula appears as a bright hyperechoic cortical line laterally with posterior acoustic shadowing.
The FHL appears as a hypoechoic muscle belly lying posteromedial to the fibula, deep to the Soleus.
Ask the patient to flex the great toe — dynamic movement confirms the FHL.
Medially, the posterior tibial vessels and tibial nerve are visible — these must be identified and avoided.
safetyNotes: The posterior tibial artery and tibial nerve lie medial to the FHL — always use a lateral approach.
The peroneal artery runs between the FHL and tibialis posterior — identify with color Doppler.
The sural nerve runs superficially in the posterior calf — avoid the superficial subcutaneous plane.

probePlacementHint: Patient prone, feet off edge. Place probe transversely on distal posterolateral calf, adjacent to fibula. Photo from behind showing probe on distal lateral calf near fibula.
pearls: Key target for great toe hyperflexion/hitchhiker's toe pattern
Located lateral to FDL in the deep posterior compartment
Small muscle, conservative dosing — over-injection causes toe weakness during push-off
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Extensor Hallucis Longus (EHL)
landmarks: Anterior Tibia (palpate the anterior tibial crest — the EHL lies in the anterior compartment lateral to the tibialis anterior)
Distal Fibula (palpate the lateral malleolus and trace the fibula proximally — the EHL originates from the middle third of the anterior fibula)
placement: Position the patient supine with the leg extended and the ankle in neutral.
Identify the distal third of the anterior leg, approximately 10 cm proximal to the ankle joint.
Ask the patient to extend (dorsiflex) the great toe — the EHL tendon will become visible and palpable on the dorsum of the foot.
Trace the tendon proximally to locate the muscle belly, which lies between the Tibialis Anterior (medially) and Extensor Digitorum Longus (laterally).
Palpate the gap between these two muscle bellies — the EHL is in this interval.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the skin into the EHL muscle belly.
Advance 1-2 cm into the muscle substance.
Aspirate before injecting.
setup: Palpate the EHL tendon during active hallux extension to trace it proximally to the muscle belly.
The EHL is a relatively small muscle — precise needle placement is important.
The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve run in the anterior compartment near the EHL — use caution.
USG can confirm placement and identify the neurovascular structures.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse across the distal anterolateral leg, approximately 10 cm proximal to the ankle
viewSteps: Identify the thin subcutaneous tissue on the anterior leg.
The Tibialis Anterior is the large hypoechoic muscle medially, adjacent to the tibial cortex.
The EHL appears as a smaller hypoechoic muscle belly between the Tibialis Anterior and the Extensor Digitorum Longus.
The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve appear as an anechoic pulsatile circle and hyperechoic structure on the interosseous membrane, adjacent to the EHL.
The fibula is visible laterally as a hyperechoic cortical line.
safetyNotes: The anterior tibial artery and deep peroneal nerve run immediately adjacent to the EHL — identify them with color Doppler before injecting.
The EHL is a small muscle; avoid injecting into the adjacent Tibialis Anterior or Extensor Digitorum Longus by confirming identity with dynamic toe extension.
The interosseous membrane lies deep — do not advance the needle to this level.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, leg extended. Place probe transversely on distal anterolateral leg, ~10 cm above ankle. Photo from front showing probe on anterior distal leg.
pearls: Causes great toe hyperextension (hitchhiker's toe/striatal toe)
Thin muscle — easy to over-inject. Start with very low doses (10-25U Botox)
Runs between tibialis anterior and EDL — identify all three on US
supplies: 25-27G needle (1.5 inch for superficial, 2-3 inch for deep)

name: Adductor Hallucis
landmarks: 1st and 2nd Metatarsal Heads — Plantar (palpate the ball of the foot under the great toe and second toe — the oblique head of adductor hallucis lies in the web space between these two metatarsals)
placement: Position the patient supine with the foot accessible, or prone with the foot dorsiflexed.
Palpate the 1st and 2nd metatarsal heads on the plantar surface — these are the bony prominences under the ball of the foot.
For the oblique head: the injection site is in the web space between the 1st and 2nd metatarsals on the plantar surface.
A dorsal approach is an alternative: insert the needle from the dorsum of the foot between the 1st and 2nd metatarsal shafts, directed plantarward.
Insert the needle perpendicular to the foot surface into the small muscle belly.
Advance 1-1.5 cm — this is a small intrinsic foot muscle.
For the transverse head: the muscle runs across the MTP joints — inject at the level of the metatarsal heads.
Aspirate before injecting. Use a small-gauge needle (25G or smaller) given the tight anatomic space.
setup: This is an advanced injection — the anatomy of the intrinsic foot muscles is compact and complex.
USG guidance is recommended to navigate the small intrinsic space and avoid neurovascular structures.
A dorsal approach may be less painful for the patient than a plantar approach.
Consider using EMG guidance as an adjunct to confirm placement in this small muscle.

ultrasound.orientation: Longitudinal along the 1st-2nd intermetatarsal space on the plantar surface, or transverse across the metatarsal heads
viewSteps: Identify the hyperechoic cortex of the 1st and 2nd metatarsal shafts as bright lines with posterior shadowing.
Between the metatarsals, the Adductor Hallucis (oblique head) appears as a small hypoechoic muscle belly in the interosseous space.
The flexor tendons may be visible as hyperechoic linear structures on the plantar side of the metatarsals.
The plantar digital nerves and arteries appear as small hyperechoic and anechoic structures running between the metatarsal heads.
safetyNotes: The plantar digital nerves run between the metatarsal heads — interdigital neuromas are common here; identify nerve tissue before injecting.
The plantar metatarsal arteries run in the interosseous spaces — use color Doppler to identify them.
The first dorsal interosseous muscle lies dorsal to the adductor hallucis — ensure you are in the correct plane.

probePlacementHint: Patient supine, foot accessible. Place probe longitudinally along 1st-2nd intermetatarsal space on plantar surface. Photo showing probe on plantar forefoot between 1st and 2nd metatarsals.
pearls: Target for hallux valgus/adduction pattern in spastic foot
Very small muscle in the plantar foot — use hockey-stick probe if available
Low doses only — 10-25U Botox typically sufficient

name: Triceps
landmarks: Acromion (palpate the bony shelf at the tip of the shoulder — follow the spine of the scapula laterally to its most lateral point)
Olecranon (palpate the prominent bony point at the posterior elbow — this is the insertion of the triceps tendon)
Posterior Midline of Humerus (draw an imaginary line from the acromion to the olecranon along the posterior arm — the muscle belly is centered here)
placement: Position the patient seated or prone with the arm resting on a support, elbow flexed to approximately 90 degrees.
Identify the posterior midpoint of the humerus (halfway between the acromion and the olecranon).
Palpate the triceps muscle belly — ask the patient to extend the elbow against resistance to confirm the bulk of the long and lateral heads.
For the long head: insert the needle into the medial portion of the posterior arm at mid-humeral level, perpendicular to the skin.
For the lateral head: insert the needle into the lateral portion of the posterior arm, slightly more proximal, perpendicular to the skin.
Advance 1.5-2 cm into the muscle belly — the triceps is a large, superficial muscle on the posterior arm.
Avoid the medial aspect of the distal arm where the ulnar nerve courses in the ulnar groove behind the medial epicondyle.
Aspirate before injecting. Distribute injections across both heads if needed, spacing sites 3-4 cm apart.
setup: Surface anatomy is usually adequate given the large, easily palpable muscle belly.
USG can help distinguish the long head from the lateral head and confirm depth.
Have the patient extend the elbow against resistance to identify and mark the muscle belly before injection.
The radial nerve courses in the spiral groove of the humerus on the posterolateral aspect — stay in the central or medial muscle belly at the mid-humeral level to avoid it.

ultrasound.orientation: Transverse (short-axis) over the posterior arm at the mid-humeral level
viewSteps: Position the patient prone or seated with the arm supported. Place the probe transversely on the posterior arm.
The Long Head of the Triceps appears as the large, prominent hypoechoic muscle — it is the most superficial and medial of the three triceps heads at the mid-arm level.
The humerus appears as a bright curved hyperechoic line with posterior acoustic shadowing deep to the triceps.
CRITICAL: The Radial Nerve appears as a hyperechoic oval structure at the deep border of the triceps, in the spiral groove of the humerus — it sits between the triceps and the bone.
